items.title: Treat system-prompt edits like code changes
items.why: Prompt tweaks can silently degrade code quality, so they need the same gating discipline as production code.
how_to_steps: Run a broad per-model eval suite for every prompt change that affects an agent or coding workflow.
Use ablations to isolate which prompt line changes quality, latency, or token use.
Soak the change before full rollout if it trades off intelligence versus speed or cost.
Roll out gradually and revert immediately if the broader eval set regresses.
items.evidence: Anthropic reports that one verbosity instruction caused a 3% drop on both Opus 4.6 and 4.7 in a broader evaluation set, and it immediately reverted the change.
items.try_monday: Put your agent prompt under version control and block merges unless the change passes both narrow and broad eval suites.

items.title: Graduate capability tests into an always-on regression suite
items.why: Once an agent can reliably do a task, keep that task in the regression bank so quality drift becomes visible immediately.
how_to_steps: Start with 20 to 50 real tasks drawn from bug reports, support tickets, or manual QA checks.
Split the bank into capability evals that are still hard and regression evals that should pass nearly 100% of the time.
Run tasks in a clean environment, use multiple trials where outputs vary, and prefer deterministic graders first.
When a capability task saturates, move it into the continuous regression suite and track latency, token use, and failure rate over time.
items.evidence: Anthropic recommends capability tasks that later graduate into a continuous regression suite and notes that fixing eval bugs plus scaffold issues moved one CORE-Bench result from 42% to 95%.
items.try_monday: Pick five agent tasks that already feel solved and convert them into a tiny must-pass regression suite.
